CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Jan-21-2015 Off Calendar

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Dec-02-2014 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On Feb-03-15 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Defendant'S Motion For Summary Judgment
Off Calendar Nov-21-2014 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. CHUCK GEERHART OF PAOLI GEERHART LLP, TEL. NO. 498-2101 EXT. 102 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. PAUL CALEO OF BURNHAM/BROWN, TEL. NO. (510)835-6809 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANTS STARBUCKS CORPORATION AND DONALD WALLIS'S Motion For Summary Judgment is denied. On June 7, 2013, plaintiff was injured when a third party patron ("patron") flew into a rage and threw a display that landed on plaintiff. A store owner owes a duty to undertake reasonable and appropriate measures to protect patrons from imminent criminal conduct. See Delgado v. Trax Bar & Grill (2005) 36 Cal.4th 224, 241. The court finds that there is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether defendant breached this duty of care by failing to ban the patron from the premises or by lying to him and refusing to process his gift card request. The evidence indicates that defendant had numerous prior encounters with the patron and defendant should have known that he posed a threat. A month prior to the incident that forms the basis of plaintiff's complaint, defendant's employee Kaira Etrata asked the patron to move because he was blocking the entrance to the premises. The patron refused to move and rudely called her "[a] five-cent ho." (Etrata Depo., 44:4-47:5). Two to three weeks prior to the incident, the patron created a mess at the premises, spoke loudly, and told defendant that "you can't tell me what to do." (Shaver Dec., par. 3; Shaver Depo., 17:19-18:9). Lastly, on the day of the incident, defendant provoked the patron by lying to him and failing to process his gift card request. (Ertata Depo., 75:21-77:16). A reasonable trier of fact could infer that defendant knew or should have known that the patron was aggressive and volatile and posed a risk to other customers at the coffee bar. The court further notes that defendant's failure to train its employees, including the shift manager, on how to deal with a disruptive customer exacerbated the situation. (Erata Depo., 33:5-9; 39:22-40:1). ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. Judge: Ernest H. Goldsmith; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sheryl L. Sawyer, CSR # 5976 =302/ehg
